title: OODA Loop

content.statement: Whoever completes the Observe-Orient-Decide-Act cycle fastest controls the tempo.
content.explainer: Speed of adaptation beats raw quality of any single decision, because a faster cycle makes your rival react to a situation that has already changed. Get inside their loop and they are forever fighting your last move.
questions: How long does it take you to go from noticing a market shift to actually shipping a response?
Where in your Observe-Orient-Decide-Act loop does the delay actually live, gathering data, interpreting it, deciding, or executing?
Which competitor forces you to react to their moves more often than you force them to react to yours?
sop: Map one recurring decision as four stages: Observe, Orient, Decide, Act.
Time how long each stage takes end to end and find your slowest one.
Rate your cycle time against your fastest competitor's cycle time (see calculation, page 6).
Cut the biggest delay by pre-deciding rules so Orient and Decide collapse into one step.
Re-run the loop weekly and track whether your tempo edge is rising.

definitions.def: Observe, Orient, Decide, Act, the four-step decision cycle developed by military strategist John Boyd to describe how you adapt to a changing situation.
The rate at which you make and execute decisions. Setting the tempo means forcing others to react to you rather than the other way around.

content.equation: Tempo Edge = Competitor Cycle Time ÷ Your Cycle Time
content.equation_example: Your rival takes 60 hours to spot a change and ship a response; you take 20 hours. 60 ÷ 20 = 3. A tempo edge above 1 means you cycle faster and set the pace; below 1 means you are the one always reacting.
content.closing_statement: The willingness to move before you feel fully ready is often what separates the people who shape events from the people who get shaped by them.
content.question: How long does it take you to go from noticing a market shift to actually shipping a response?

caption:
Principle Nº 355: OODA Loop

Whoever completes the Observe-Orient-Decide-Act cycle fastest controls the tempo.

Ask yourself: How long does it take you to go from noticing a market shift to actually shipping a response?
